item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article vaguely reminded me of this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm going to have nightmares about that final log.

+1.

item.title: Re: SCP-3033
item.content:encoded:
Ever since I've read this article, I have been disturbed by the ending ever since.
Is there a tale where that woman lost her value and was

continue 
close 

terminated? Like the Foundation somehow managed to get everything they wanted from her, so she was given a notice of her incoming termination, and she like just shrugged it off cause it will be a quick death. On the day of her termination, she was secretly kidnapped by a high ranking personnel, because the personnel wanted to take revenge of her. So the kidnapper wanted to throw her in the incinerator and before she was about to be thrown, the woman just smiled and mocked him about being petty and stupid, and that being burned alive is not that big of a deal. Then the kidnapper suddenly stab her with syringe and injected something inside her and says," congratulations, you're now immortal" and just pushed that b████ in the incinerator.

item.title: Re: SCP-3033
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for necroposting, but after seeing the poor woman die in a attempt to kill Craggs.
I seriously want to see Craggs die… Seriously… If you ever decide to let go of that character i will read her end…
+1 for the hate induced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was an intended stylistic choice. E-1261 is at most an older teenager, and has been a test subject since being a preteen, from a relatively poorer part of the world. It's a small miracle she can form something resembling cohesive sentences over a computer terminal - most other subjects are functionally brain-dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second interview log thing lacked any capitalisation and grammar. I'll take the liberty of correcting it for you, since it's all inconsequential stuff like writing "ill" instead of "I'll".

Feel free to yell at me if I fuck everything up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, here's the way I wrote it. Multiple interpretations are possible.

E-1260 and E-1261 are sleeper agents for the Chaos Insurgency. However, E-1261 is a reluctant, and unaware member of this operation, as prisoner-test subject-'Mike'. To complicate things, 1261 blames the Chaos Insurgency and especially Craggs for making her this way.

The initial breakout of Foundation custody is with 1260 controlling/proxying through 1261 - up until the point that 1261 collapses the first time. Craggs has known about this the entire time, and planned ahead - all the controllers can ALSO be controlled, directly, by Craggs, through the same technology. Craggs forces 1260 to strangle himself, assuming that 1261 is basically catatonic without a controller.

Instead, 1261 wakes up, realizes the opportunity she has, and lunges at Craggs on her own initiative - which Craggs, always being one step ahead, thwarts anyway.

In short, "No one leaves the Chaos Insurgency." are words from 1260, proxying through 1261. If you check the transcripts, E-1261 never swears, ever, until the last document. Check the dates. If this is present day, she went through all of this when she was barely a teenager. It's supposed to be a radical change of tone to indicate that this actually isn't 1261.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how you have us fighting with ourselves whether to root for Craggs or E-1261. Each is a hero and a villain in their own way, and how 1261 is a decent person doing the wrong thing and Craggs is a horrible person working for the good guys is so messed up. This is one of the few SCPs I've ever read where the characters and story are what make it instead of the item, and they're amazingly written. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, definite +1.
Secondly, am I misunderstanding the nature of E-1261? Because the overall impression I got was that she was a sleeper agent for the Insurgency, and used to eliminate E-1260, then Craggs. Her whole "backstory" instead becomes a sob story in order to get the foundation to drop their guard just enough to let her dispose of E-1260 (the real victim out of the two, and one who might reveal further information), and proceed to dispose of Craggs for revealing project secrets. Now, I'm basing this off of E-1261's statement: "No one leaves the Chaos Insurgency." Based on the other comments here though, I'm trying to clarify whether my interpretation is correct or not. Great read, regardless.

item.title: Re: SCP-3033
item.content:encoded:
This article (specifically, the fate of the character in the last collapsible) fucked me up emotionally for several days after reading it.

+1 for that alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless they're a threat to the existence of humanity, they aren't Keter. This is probably Euclid.

Keter is more of "very difficult to contain", not necessarily danger to humanity. There are Safe SCPs that pose a strong threat to humanity, but they are Safe since they will not endanger humanity unless someone does something to it.

For a Keter, even if no one does anything to it, it will still break out and do whatever it wishes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless they're a threat to the existence of humanity, they aren't Keter. This is probably Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1'd hard dude
The buildup to vengeance was great, and it was quite refreshing and at the same time frustrating (in a good way) to see E-1261 ultimately fail in what, if the Foundation universe was full of happy endings, should have been their long-awaited vengeance. To have the Foundation simply move Craggs elsewhere is infuriatingly in-character for them (if a nebulous organisation such as the Foundation can be considered to have 'character'). Most articles don't make me feel angry at the Foundation, but this is a well-earned exception to the rule.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They don't fit very nicely into a category - if they're already contained, it's not too much of an issue as they seem fairly docile. They issue is that there's lots running around uncontained, and they're difficult to kill, much less capture.

If you can find a good justification for Euclid or Safe, I'm all ears. Given the difficulty in capturing one, it seemed like a Keter to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not entirely sure why these are Keter

item.title: Re: SCP-3033
item.content:encoded:
shame on you for linking a crappy article

mine is no good compared to this :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edits made as suggested. E-1261 second interview trimmed down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really want to upvote for a well-written villain. Craggs is utterly reprehensible, but quite affable at the same time; she's definitely the star of this show.

My issue lies with E-1261's backstory explanation: It's far too showy, to the point of being overwrought. I feel so overwhelmed with the bite-sized stream of torture that I just end up feeling numb, and wanting to scroll. I think it seems a little forced, and already covers things one could glean from exposition given by the other characters or the scenario in general.

I suggest moving through her incarceration/experimentation in a less literal/direct fashion. More haze, confusion, lost periods of time. Sensations. Only being able to recount things on a fringe-level. Imply the conditions, rather than list them off. Then, the scene where she kills, of course, can be as vivid as-is currently written (as she remembers these events distinctly). This kind of tone-down would move this away from (imo) trying too hard to be 'horrible'. (Also, the 'I watched myself…I did those things' bit is a little over-the-top for my tastes as well.)

This would have secured a downvote, but I'll be damned if Craggs doesn't more than make up for it. Neutral.

ETA: Whelp, that solved my problems! Good job on quite possibly the only CI character I've ever liked (not that I've read a ton of them, but still). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminded me of the Unit 731 personnel who were granted immunity by the U.S. after the war.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dear lord. The whole article is this disturbing lab experiment explained in excruciating detail. And that whole build up of E-1261 finally getting revenge only to have her once again screwed with by the same pyscopath she was attempting to kill? Horrific. Wonderfully made. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just awful in all the right ways. +1.

item.title: SCP-3033
item.content:encoded:
So, finally got around to getting this posted. Started life as a 3000 draft, posted as a mainlister.

Craggs probably has to be one of my favorite characters I've ever written for anything.

Thanks to weryllium, ihp, Conwell, psul, and the dozens of other people that looked at this in its exceptionally long drafting period.

EDIT: I nearly forgot. Special thanks for Noktigo for inspiring me to write this with SCP-2715
